title: Water into Wine: The First Sign
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
series: The First Year of Jesus' Ministry

outline.title: Introduction: The Gospel of John and Its Seven Signs
subpoints: John is unique among the four gospels: almost no OT quotations yet filled with OT themes; chronologically precise via feast calendar
John's agenda: the man Jesus Christ is nothing less than the incarnate God — fully man, fully God (John 1:14)
Seven signs structure John's gospel — not called 'miracles' but 'signs' because they point beyond themselves to Christ (John 20:30-31)

outline.title: 1. What Is a Sign?
subpoints: A sign is an event that points to someone else — it doesn't exist for itself but always to point beyond
We don't marvel at the sign but at the one to whom the sign points — this is why Jesus refused to perform signs on demand (the religious leaders wanted the trick, not Christ)
John 2:11: 'He manifested his glory and his disciples believed in him' — the goal is faith in Christ

outline.title: 2. The Wedding Setting and Mary's Request (vv. 1-5)
subpoints: A Jewish wedding could last seven days — the groom was responsible to provide everything; running out of wine brought lasting shame and could result in legal action from the bride's family
Mary's implicit request to Jesus — though this is his first recorded miracle, she came to him with confidence, perhaps based on the events around his birth or current reliance on her eldest son
Jesus' response: 'Woman, what does your concern have to do with me? My hour has not yet come' — Jesus transcends the conversation to the spiritual plane

outline.title: 3. The Deeper Meaning of the Hour and the Groom
subpoints: Jesus' 'hour' throughout John refers to the cross: 'before the feast of the Passover, when Jesus knew that his hour had come' (John 13:1)
The groom at a wedding is responsible for the wine — Jesus says it is not yet his hour to be THE groom because that happens at the cross
At the cross, Jesus married his bride the church and provided the richest wine — his own blood, the new covenant (Luke 22:20)
The cup of wrath he drank; the sweet wine of fellowship with his bride awaits at the marriage supper of the Lamb (Revelation 19:7)

outline.title: 4. The Miracle: Water to Wine (vv. 6-10)
subpoints: Six stone water pots for purification, each 20-30 gallons — filled completely to the brim with water, leaving no room for speculation that it was diluted
The use of purification jars is theologically significant: the wine of Christ's blood does what ceremonial purification laws could not — it truly purifies
Yet he uses the same jars — his ministry is a continuation of what God was doing in the Old Testament, not a rejection of it
The wine was better and more abundant than what came before — true of everything about Jesus; he transforms the old watered-down self into the rich wine of the new creation (2 Cor. 5:17)

outline.title: Application: Drink of the Wine of His Grace
subpoints: The groom at the feast had no idea what happened — 'this is just like our salvation: Jesus does the work, we take the credit by faith'
Isaiah 55:1: 'Come, everyone who thirsts, come to the waters; and you who have no money, come, buy and eat'
Being drunk with Christ, not with wine — be filled with him, heart and mind and body, and then reflect his grace and glory to those around you

summary: Continuing a series on the first year of Jesus' public ministry, Pastor Tito Lyro examines John 2:1-11 — Jesus' first recorded sign, turning water into wine at a wedding in Cana. The sermon explores why John calls miracles 'signs' (they point beyond themselves to Christ), the cultural stakes of the wedding (a failed feast would bring lasting shame and legal liability), the theological significance of Jesus' 'hour' not yet having come (it refers to the cross, where he becomes the ultimate Groom providing the wine of his blood), and the symbolism of using purification jars (Christ does what ceremonial law could not — truly purifies). The wine was better and more abundant than anything before — as is everything about Christ.
transcript:
## Introduction: The First Sign

We're continuing our series on the first year of Jesus' public ministry. From this point we'll be largely in the Gospel of John, because John gives us the most details about that first year — particularly John 1-4.

The Gospel of John is the most unique of the four gospels. It has almost no quotations from the Old Testament, and yet it is filled with Old Testament themes — if you don't understand the Old Testament you'll have a hard time understanding John, even though he has only one-tenth the OT quotations that Matthew has. Much of what is common to Matthew, Mark, and Luke is not in John; John fills the gaps left by the other three. He writes perhaps 30 years later. And he organizes his account around seven signs — seven miracles — which structure the three years of Jesus' life as John presents them.

Here in chapter 2: water to wine. In chapter 4: healing the nobleman's son. Chapter 5: healing the paralytic at Bethesda. Chapter 6: feeding the five thousand (the only miracle in all four gospels) and walking on water. Chapter 9: giving sight to the man born blind. Chapter 11: raising Lazarus. Seven signs. John 20:30-31 tells us why he selected these: *These are written that you may believe that Jesus is the Christ, the Son of God, and that believing you may have life in his name.*

## What Is a Sign?

Why does John use the word *sign* instead of *miracle*? A sign is an event that points beyond itself to someone else. A sign doesn't exist for its own sake — it always points to a destination. When you're on the freeway and you start seeing signs for your exit, you're excited — but once you arrive, you don't say, 'I'll go back to look at those signs.' The signs served their purpose.

This is why Jesus refused to perform miracles when religious leaders demanded them. They wanted to see the trick, the spectacular event — they didn't understand that the sign was designed to point to the one doing the signing. We don't marvel at the signs; we marvel at the one performing them, the Lord Jesus Christ.

John 2:11 gives us the whole point: *This beginning of signs Jesus did in Cana of Galilee, and manifested his glory, and his disciples believed in him.* Signs → manifestation of glory → belief in Christ.

## The Wedding at Cana (vv. 1-5)

A Jewish wedding could last up to seven days. It began with a procession from the groom's house to the bride's house, then back to the groom's house where the feast was held. The groom was responsible to provide everything. And here is what makes this situation so serious for this couple: if the groom failed to provide an adequate feast, the guests could take back up to half their gifts, and the bride's family could sue him. This couple would have walked through the market for the rest of their lives carrying the shame of a failed wedding feast. Jesus rescued them.

Mary, the mother of Jesus, was probably a relative of the family — she had inside knowledge about the supply running out. Her request is implicit: *They have no wine.* She is asking Jesus to do something.

Jesus' response in verse 4 is the most difficult verse in the passage: *Woman, what does your concern have to do with me? My hour has not yet come.* He is not being dismissive of Mary — he is transcending the conversation. Throughout John, 'my hour' refers to the cross: *Before the feast of the Passover, when Jesus knew that his hour had come* (John 13:1). What Jesus is saying is this: *The hour for me to be the Groom is not yet here.* And yet — Mary sees in that answer reason to believe he will act. She tells the servants: *Whatever he says to you, do it.*

## The Deeper Meaning: The Hour and the Groom

At a Jewish wedding, the groom provides the wine. Jesus is saying: *My wedding — where I will provide the ultimate wine — is still coming.* When is that hour? The cross. At the cross Jesus married his bride the church. He provided the richest wine of all — his own blood, the new covenant: *This cup is the new covenant in my blood, which is shed for you* (Luke 22:20). What is a marriage but a covenant? He drank the bitter wine of the Father's wrath on your behalf, so that at his return he may drink the sweet wine of fellowship with his bride — *the marriage of the Lamb has come, and his wife has made herself ready* (Revelation 19:7).

To prove that he will do just that on the cross, he goes ahead and provides the wine for this wedding.

## The Miracle Itself (vv. 6-10)

Six stone water pots for purification — the ceremonial hand-washing the Jews practiced before meals. Each held 20-30 gallons, all empty. Jesus said: *Fill them to the brim.* The servants filled them completely — no room for speculation about dilution. It was water from top to bottom. Then draw some out and take it to the master of the feast — and it is wine. Better wine than anything they had served before.

The use of purification jars is not accidental. The Holy Spirit is telling us something. Jesus is using jars for ritual purification to show that he is the one who truly purifies — doing what the ceremonial laws of purification could never do. His blood accomplishes what those water-jars could only symbolize. And yet he uses the *same* jars — showing continuity with what God was doing in the Old Testament. His ministry is fulfillment, not rejection.

The wine was better and more abundant. This is true of everything about Jesus. 2 Corinthians 5:17: *If anyone is in Christ, he is a new creation — old things have passed away, behold, all things have become new.* From his fullness we have received *grace upon grace* (John 1:16). He transforms the old watering-pots of our former selves into the rich wine of the new creation.

## Application: Drink of His Grace

The groom at the feast had no idea what happened. The master came to congratulate him on keeping the best wine until last — and he had no idea. This is like our salvation: Jesus does the work; we take the credit by faith. He wants us to consider his death our death, his resurrection our resurrection.

Isaiah 55:1: *Ho! Everyone who thirsts, come to the waters; and you who have no money, come, buy and eat. Yes, come, buy wine and milk without money and without price.*

Christ calls us to drink of the wine of his grace. Not to be drunk with wine or substances — but to be so filled with Christ, so saturated with his grace and his glory, that we become people who reflect his grace and glory to those around us.

Father, thank you for your Word. We pray that through the foolishness of the message proclaimed you see fit to glorify yourself in our midst. For those who are in Christ — help us to grow in love with him, to see how glorious he is. For those who are not — we pray that they would come to him, embrace him, drink of him, even to the salvation of their souls. In Jesus' name, amen.
